front: What is the definition of a Group in algebra?
back: A group is an algebraic structure (G, *) consisting of a set G and a binary operation * that satisfies four axioms: closure (for all a, b in G, a*b is in G), associativity (for all a, b, c in G, (a*b)*c = a*(b*c)), identity (there exists an element e in G such that for every a in G, e*a = a*e = a), and invertibility (for each a in G, there exists an element b in G such that a*b = b*a = e, where e is the identity).

front: What is an Abelian (or commutative) Group?
back: An Abelian group is a group (G, *) where the operation * is commutative. That is, for all elements a and b in G, a * b = b * a. Examples include the set of integers under addition and the set of real numbers (excluding zero) under multiplication.

front: What is the Order of a Group?
back: The order of a group (G, *) is the number of elements in its set G. It is denoted by |G|. A group can have finite order (e.g., the group of integers modulo n under addition) or infinite order (e.g., the group of all integers under addition).

A subgroup (H, *) of a group (G, *) is a subset H of G that itself forms a group under the same operation *. This means H must be closed under the operation and taking inverses, and must contain the identity element of G. For example, the set of even integers is a subgroup of the integers under addition.

A cyclic group is a group that can be generated by a single element. This means every element in the group can be written as a power (or multiple, if the operation is addition) of this generator. If G = ⟨a⟩, then G = { aⁿ | n is an integer }. All cyclic groups are Abelian.
